coredata.dc:title: Two-dimensional metal-organic framework nanoleaf embedded with nitrogen-doped carbon dots for monitoring of picric acid

coredata.dc:description: A novel preparation of 2D metal–organic framework (Zn-MOF, ZIF-8) nanoleaf (ZIF-L) embedded with nitrogen-doped carbon dots (NCDs) was applied successfully. The NCDs are synthesized through hydrothermal treatment of citric acid and ethylenediamine exhibiting excitation wavelength independence (λex-independence) of 6 nm and higher quantum yield reaches 84 %. The ZIF-L embedded with NCDs generates a 2D continuous framework of NCDs@ ZIF-L nanocomposite. The nanocomposite was fully characterized by FTIR, XRD, TEM, and N2 adsorption-desorption mechanism. The XRD data reveals no significant change in the crystallinity of ZIF-L upon the encapsulation of NCDs, but it enhances the fluorescence emission. The synergetic cooperation between ZIF-L and NCDs might free active sites that lead to higher adsorption capacity and thus enhance their application in chemical sensing. The NCDs@ZIF-L nanocomposite synergistically combines active-rich-electron nanospaces with a high surface area, both of which are particularly beneficial for the selective detection of hazardous picric acid (PA) because of reinforced hydrogen bonding interactions with the hydroxyl groups of PA aromatic rings. The NCDs@ZIF-L framework is utilized to monitor PA as a nitro explosive compound in aqueous media exhibiting a high sensing activity and selectivity to detect PA in diluted solutions with a low detection limit (LOD) reaching 0.139 µM. The nanocomposite was also utilized in real water samples spiked with different concentrations of PA in tap water, with R % reaching 91.3–102 % and RSD % less than 5 %, showing their applicability to detect PA under circumstances.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A novel preparation of 2D metal–organic framework (Zn-MOF, ZIF-8) nanoleaf (ZIF-L) embedded with nitrogen-doped carbon dots (NCDs) was applied successfully. The NCDs are synthesized through hydrothermal treatment of citric acid and ethylenediamine exhibiting excitation wavelength independence (λex-independence) of 6 nm and higher quantum yield reaches 84 %. The ZIF-L embedded with NCDs generates a 2D continuous framework of NCDs@ ZIF-L nanocomposite. The nanocomposite was fully characterized by FTIR, XRD, TEM, and N2 adsorption-desorption mechanism. The XRD data reveals no significant change in the crystallinity of ZIF-L upon the encapsulation of NCDs, but it enhances the fluorescence emission. The synergetic cooperation between ZIF-L and NCDs might free active sites that lead to higher adsorption capacity and thus enhance their application in chemical sensing. The NCDs@ZIF-L nanocomposite synergistically combines active-rich-electron nanospaces with a high surface area, both of which are particularly beneficial for the selective detection of hazardous picric acid (PA) because of reinforced hydrogen bonding interactions with the hydroxyl groups of PA aromatic rings. The NCDs@ZIF-L framework is utilized to monitor PA as a nitro explosive compound in aqueous media exhibiting a high sensing activity and selectivity to detect PA in diluted solutions with a low detection limit (LOD) reaching 0.139 µM. The nanocomposite was also utilized in real water samples spiked with different concentrations of PA in tap water, with R % reaching 91.3–102 % and RSD % less than 5 %, showing their applicability to detect PA under circumstances.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: The environmental pollution has become a serious concern that poses a critical threat to human safety and the surrounding environment, especially the use of nitroexplosive compounds that are harmful to human beings [1,2]. Nitroaromatic compounds (NACs) as 2,4,6-trinitrotoluene (TNT), nitrobenzene (NB), 1,3-dinitrobenzene (1,3-DNB), and 2,4,6-trinitrophenol (TNP) also named as picric acid (PA) are recognized as dangerous explosive compounds [3,4]. Picric acid is known as one of the most powerful explosives due to its strong electron withdrawing properties [5]. It has been utilized as a material ingredient in military activities and in chemical and pharmaceutical industries as dyes and pesticides. On the other hand, these industries are not highly desirable because they are the main source of industrial wastes [6–8] due to their high toxicity and explosive features that cause detrimental concerns on human health since they are carcinogenic, water soluble, and non-degradable [9,10] which lead to contamination of soil and ground water. As a result, it might cause health issues such as eye and skin irritation when inhaled, damage of liver function, dizziness, and respiratory disorders [11,12]. Based on the aforementioned characteristics, the rapid and sensitive detection of PA has become crucial to monitor their levels in the environment [7]. The traditional methods applied for PA detection are Raman spectroscopy [13], gas chromatography (GC) [14], infrared spectroscopy [15], mass spectrometry [16], and fluorescence spectroscopy [17]. However, most of these techniques are time consuming, expensive, have low sensitivity, and longtime analysis [7,9]. Thus, the development of rapid, high sensitive techniques and efficient methods for sensing PA traces in water has become of high necessity [10].

Among them, fluorescence has proved its ability to be one of the most impressive techniques in sensing nitroexplosive contamination for its low cost, high selectivity and sensitivity, and high response in stable water [18]. Since then, many fluorescent probes have been designed based on different fluorescent materials, such as metal–organic frameworks (MOFs) that have been recognized as new crystalline porous materials. They act as luminescent sensors when interacting with other analytes [2,19]. The study of nanoporous MOFs showed considerable interest due to their intrinsic properties as flexible morphology of different functionalities, high surface area, high crystallinity, large pore volumes, and high chemical stability and, thus, emerged as promising materials for sensing explosives [20,21]. Zeolitic imidazole frameworks (ZIFs) are subclass of MOFs that consist of metal ions connected to imidazolate linkers with a specific arrangement of crystalline network [22]. ZIF-8 is a three-dimensional material with sodalite morphology; they are formed by linkage of Zn2+ metal ions and 2-methyl imidazole (Hmim) of surface area reaching 1700 m2 g−1 and pore diameter 11.6 Å [20,23]. They display unique properties of tunable porosity, high thermal and chemical stability, hydrophobicity, and abundant functionalities [24,25]. This leads to using them in different fields, including catalysis [26], water treatment [27], chemical sensing [28], and drug delivery [29]. ZIFs can be produced through different processes such as solvothermal process. However, the time consumption and the usage of organic solvents that are mostly expensive made it difficult to produce pure ZIF-8 crystals and thus limited their use in many applications. Therefore, finding an approach for the preparation of ZIFs of low cost and environmentally friendly has been a great challenge for researchers to synthesize ZIFs in aqueous media at room temperature [30,31].

Pan et al. [32] have reported the preparation of ZIF-8 crystals in aqueous solution at room temperature with higher thermal stability. On the other hand, a new type of two-dimensional (2D) ZIF was reported to have leaf shaped like structure and cushion shaped cavity that attracts a good attention due to their unique properties with exposed active sites [33,34]. Wang et al. [35] were the first to synthesize a novel 2D ZIF crystalline material named ZIF-L that showed excellent CO2 adsorption using Zn salt and 2-methylimidazole (Hmim) in an aqueous solution at ambient temperature with molar ratio Zn(mim)2.(Hmim)1/2.(H2O)3/2 that possessed a unique leaf-like crystal morphology of micropore volume 0.066 cm3 g−1 and surface area 161 m2 g−1 lower than ZIF-8. It has been reported that ZIF-L possesses a 2D crystal morphology within its layers in the form of semi-sodalite structure and contains a free monodentate Hmim group linked through hydrogen bonds, unlike ZIF-8 where all Zn ions are connected to bidentate Hmim ligands through deprotonation of imidazole linkers [36,37]. This allows the surface of ZIF-L to have more active sites and, hence, a decrease in BET surface area, which could enhance the interaction for sensing applications [38]. These results of increasing interest in ZIF-L are due to their unique properties for the synthesis of 2D material composites. For example, Lu et al. [38] have synthesized ZIF-L decorated with multi-walled carbon nanotubes (MWCNTs/ZIF-L) composite material for electrochemical sensing of thiabendazole. Some active materials are incorporated into MOFs as oxides, carbon quantum dots (CDs), polymers, etc. [39].

Carbon quantum dots are sp2-conjugated cores with a diameter of less than 10 nm [40]. They have gained attention as attractive fluorescent materials due to their unique properties, such as their outstanding chemical stability, hydrophilicity, low toxicity, biocompatibility, and superb fluorophores with tunable emission [41–43]. CDs possess functionalized surfaces containing oxygen and nitrogen functional groups that can interact with other organic species and hence are featured as fluorescence nanomaterial [44]. They can be obtained by several methods, such as pyrolysis [45], laser ablation [46], hydrothermal treatment [47], and combustion [48]. However, these methods showed drawbacks due to the complicated equipment applied, high cost, and high energy consumption [49]. The development of simple techniques with low consumption, one-step preparation, and environment-friendly, such as hydrothermal route based on water systems, has been recently reported [8,50]. Due to their intrinsic properties, they have been explored in different applications such as catalysis [51], drug delivery [52], chemical sensing [53], and optoelectronic devices [54]. The incorporation of CDs into MOFs formed nanocomposites could enhance the fluorescence properties, specifically in sensing applications. For example, Lin et al. [55] succeeded in encapsulating branched poly-(ethylenimine)-capped CDs into ZIF-8 for sensitive detection of copper ions (Cu2+) of low detection limit (LOD) 80 pM.

Herein, we report a synthesis of NCDs@ZIF-L nanocomposite with a rough surface and a high quantum yield reaches 84 % through the deposition of NCDs of size 6 nm synthesized from citric acid and ethylene diamine and ZIF-L using a green approach in aqueous media at room temperature. The construction of NCDs@ZIF-L is expected to address the shortcomings of single materials through synergistic interactions and promote further development in more research areas. To the best of our knowledge, NCDs@ZIF-L has rarely been explored in sensing. The synthesized NCDs@ZIF-L nanocomposite synergistically combines active-rich-electron nanospaces with a high surface area, both of which are particularly beneficial for selective detection of hazardous PA. The nanocomposite exhibits a remarkable increase in its quenching, demonstrating its high sensitivity and selectivity towards PA with a very low LOD of 0.139 μM at room temperature within the range of PA of 0.099–2.75 μM, despite the presence of other analytes interferences. The resultant 2D nanocomposite framework possesses more active adsorption sites that dramatically increase their adsorption capability towards PA. Further, the nanocomposite shows an enhancement in the fluorescence emission, thus enhances the sensing affinity towards PA.

All the chemicals are of analytical grade and used without further purification. Citric acid (99.5 %) was purchased from (AppliChem, Germany). Ethylenediamine (99 %) and 2-Methylimidazole (Hmim, 99%) were obtained from (ACROS ORGANICS, Belgium). Zinc nitrate hexahydrate (Zn(NO3)2·6H2O, purity 99 %, LANXESS, Germany), and methanol (HPLC-grade, CHEMLAP, Belgium) were used as received. Picric acid (PA) was obtained from Fisher Scientific UK. Hydrochloric acid (37 %, EMSURE, Germany) and sodium hydroxide (Fisher Chemicals, United Kingdom) were used to adjust the pH of nanocomposite solutions. Different organic solvents, such as tetrahydrofuran (THF), toluene (Tol), acetic acid (Ac), methanol (MeOH), and ethanol (EtOH) were used to test the composite selectivity towards them. For measuring the selectivity and sensitivity of NCDs@ZIF-L nanocomposite towards PA, different interferences such as heavy metals, aromatic, and nitro compounds were studied. For aromatic interferences, 3-amino phenol (3-AP), 4-amino phenol (4-AP), benzene (B), toluene (Tol), resorcinol (Res), p-nitro phenol (PNP), nitrobenzene (NB), and o-chlorophenol (o-ClP). Metal ions such as Co2+, Fe2+, Fe3+, Ni2+, Pd2+, Pt4+, Ca2+, Cu2+, K+, Mg2+, Na+, Sn2+, and Zn2+ were also used as interfering elements to study selectivity tests of NCDs@ZIF-L nanocomposite towards PA.

Fourier transform infrared spectra (FTIR) were accomplished by Bruker VERTEX 70v connected to platinum (ATR model V-100) in the wave number (500–4000 cm−1). Transmission Electron Microscope (TEM) images were measured under JEM 2100F, JEOL to investigate the morphology of NCDs and ZIF-L. X-ray diffraction spectrum (XRD) was detected using Shimadzu XRD-6100 with 3 kW maximum output with Cu radiation source (λ = 1.54 Å) to determine the material crystalline structure. UV–vis spectrometer (Shimadzu-2600) was used to measure the absorbance in the wavelength range of 200–800 nm. The fluorescence measurements were carried out by Hitachi F-2700 with Xenon lamp light source (Xe) of 150 W, and the excitation wavelength was set to 360 nm. N2 adsorption isotherms were determined using BEL Japan (BELSORP II mini) and analyzed using the Brunauer–Emmett–Teller (BET) and Barrett–Joyner–Halenda (BJH) methods to calculate surface area, mean pore diameter, and total pore volume. The fluorescence decay was recorded by a fluorescence lifetime measuring system (Horiba FluoroCube 3000U-EYM1) equipped with a laser source set at 372 nm.

The NCDs were prepared according to a modified procedure of Sun et al. [56]. Typically, 1.05 g of citric acid and 335 mL of ethylenediamine were dissolved in 10 mL of deionized water. The solution was transferred to a Teflon-lined autoclave and heated at 200 °C for 5 h. The reactor was then cooled down to room temperature. A yellow color solution was obtained followed by centrifugation to remove the agglomerated particles.

ZIF-L was synthesized according to recently reported literature [38,57]. Briefly, a precursor solution was prepared by dissolving 2.4 g of Zn(NO3)2·6H2O in 150 mL of deionized water (DI) and 5.2 g Hmim in 150 mL DI water. Subsequently, the two solutions were mixed followed by vigorous stirring for 4 h. The resulting white powder was collected by centrifugation at 4000 rpm, thoroughly washed, and then dried under vacuum at 60 °C overnight.

The NCDs@ZIF-L nanocomposite was prepared according to a modified, published report [58]. Accordingly, 100 mg of NCDs were added to 2.4 g of Zn(NO3)2·6H2O in 150 mL DI water and magnetically stirred at room temperature. Afterward, the solution was mixed with 5.2 g of Hmim dissolved in 150 mL of DI water for 4 h. The obtained white powder was thoroughly washed with DI water and collected by centrifuge at 4000 rpm. The powder was finally dried in a vacuum oven at 60 °C overnight.

Quantum yield (QY) of NCDs was measured using quinine sulfate (QS) in 0.1 M H2SO4 as a standard reference (QY=0.54) at the excitation wavelength of 360 nm. QY measurements were calculated using Eq. (1).

Different concentrations of PA in the range of 0–5 µM were added to an aqueous solution of the NCDs@ZIF-L nanocomposite suspension (500 µL, 4 mg mL−1) composite aqueous solutions. The fluorescence intensity was measured at λex = 360 nm. With continuous addition of PA concentration, the quenching showed an increased behavior in fluorescence emission that is better described as a linear relationship by Stern-Volmer Eq. (2).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of PA in real water samples
ce:section.ce:para:
PA was spiked at different concentrations in tap water with the presence of an aqueous solution containing colloidal suspension of NCDs@ZIF-L nanocomposite, and the data was recorded at room temperature. The recovery procedure (R %) was applied with triplicate runs (n = 3) as estimated from Eq. (3).
(3)

For an enhanced sensing affinity towards traces of PA in aqueous media, the optimum conditions of the NCDs@ZIF-L nanocomposite were carefully studied. Firstly, the NCDs were hydrothermally synthesized with a mixture of citric acid and ethylenediamine. The intensity of fluorescence emission was measured, and found to be an excitation-independent wavelength. The highest emission is obtained at λex = 360 nm, gradually decreasing at higher wavelengths (Fig. 1 a, b). The QY was measured for the prepared NCDs by using QS as a reference in 0.1 M H2SO4 at λex = 360 nm, exhibiting a high QY of 84 %. We measured the fluorescence emission of NCDs and the NCDs@ZIF-L nanocomposite at λex = 360 nm and the nanocomposite shows a decrease in the emission intensity compared to pristine NCDs (Fig. 1c). It is worth noting that the fluorescence intensity is controlled by the amount of NCDs present in the ZIF-L. However, changing the condition parameters might enhance their fluorescence for chemical sensing. Thus, optimization is required to enhance the sensing activity of nanocomposite towards the targeted analyte. Several conditions were considered to prepare a selective nanomaterial for trace analysis determination of PA. The change in the NCDs@ZIF-L nanocomposite concentrations in the range of 1–8 mg mL−1 was studied using fluorescence spectra. As shown in Fig. 1d, a distinctive increase in the fluorescence intensity of the nanocomposite is realized at 4 mg mL−1.

The effect of pH on the fluorescence efficiency of NCDs and NCDs@ZIF-L nanocomposite was carefully studied at different pH ranges of 1–10. Obviously, from Fig. S1, the pH of the solution greatly affects the fluorescence intensity of the nanocomposite, implying that the solution pH is an important key factor that dramatically affects the sensing capability of the sensor. As shown in Fig. S1a, the pH of the NCDs solution significantly impacts the fluorescence intensity; the emission of NCDs was improved up to pH 5 and then slightly decreased to pH 10. This may be because the functional groups on the surface of NCDs change into negatively charged ions in an alkaline environment (high pH), where the hydroxyl and carboxyl groups may form hydrogen bonds that would account for the dependence of NCDs on pH media [59].

In the case of the NCDs@ZIF-L nanocomposite, coordination occurs between Zn2+ ions of ZIF-L and N-containing groups on the surface of the NCDs. At pH 5, the fluorescence intensity of the nanocomposite solution increases, and it gradually decreases as the pH rises to pH 10 (Fig. S1b). Naked-eye detection of the solution turbidity at pH 6 indicated that the nanocomposite material was decomposed in aqueous solution. However, the decomposition of the nanocomposite took place and the turbidity dropped at pH<6. It has been noted that a metal–ligand complexation builds up by Zn2+ nodes and Hmim linkers; however, they are unstable under acidic conditions depending on the amount of Hmim; at lower pH, they get protonated, which leads to an increase in the free Hmim concentrations where a partial replacement by solvent species occurs and the surface become dominated by water and OH groups as explained by Morozov and coworkers [60]. The dissolution of ZIF-L in water is reported in Scheme S1. As a result, the interaction between Zn2+ ions and NCDs becomes weaker, resulting in the decomposition of nanocomposite and release of incorporated NCDs, leading to a change in fluorescence intensity over time. Therefore, we carried out our sensing experiments of PA using the nanocomposite at neural pH 7, which is also close to the pH of tap water.

The optical properties of aqueous suspensions of NCDs and the NCDs@ZIF-L nanocomposite were further investigated using UV–vis absorption. The UV–vis spectrum of NCDs contains a broad absorption peak at around 350 nm with its lower absorbance edge at 417 nm (Fig. 2 ) due to the presence of C=C (π–π* transitions) and C=O (n–π*transitions), respectively [39]. Both ZIF-L and NCDs@ZIF-L nanocomposite showed two peaks at 304 and 400 nm due to the presence of an organic linker (Hmim) in ZIF-L [61]. Similarly, the NCDs@ZIF-L nanocomposite shows two absorption peaks at approximately 300 and 400 nm, where an overlap might occur between NCDs and ZIF-L [39]. The lifetime decay was also measured with a laser source set at 372 nm as explained in Fig. 2b. The decay curves are fitted to a bi-exponential function, as shown in Eq. (4) [62].

avg = 0.97 ns, which is approx. 9.8 times lower than NCDs, probably due to the low concentration of encapsulated NCDs into ZIF-L during nanocomposite synthesis. Such a decrease in the lifetime of the NCDs@ZIF-L nanocomposite is ascribed to the charge or energy transfer between NCDs and ZIF-L (Fig. 2b). In order to demonstrate energy transfer from NCDs to the ZIF-L quantitatively, the fluorescence decays that fit (Fig. 2b) with a bi-exponential function, Eq. (4) and their determined lifetimes are key factors in studying the mechanism of energy transfer that occurs throughout the nanocomposite. The fluorescence lifetime of the NCDs@ZIF-L nanocomposite was measured and is given by Eqs. (5)–(7).

where kr and knr are radiative and non-radiative decay constants, keng is the rate constant of energy transfer, and the energy transfer quantum yield is identified as φeng . From the plot in Fig. 2b, it can be seen that the energy transfer time acquired from NCDs to the ZIF-L is 1.07 ns, giving a distinguished energy transfer quantum yield (φeng ) reaches 90 %, resulting in an efficient energy transfer from NCDs to the ZIF-L could be occurred during synthesis, and a coordination complexation is accomplished between the amino groups of NCDs and Zn2+ metal ion on the external surface of ZIF-L.

High resolution TEM (HRTEM) images of NCDs (Fig. 3 a-c) reveal that NCDs have a uniform size diameter of 6 nm with a lattice fringe of 0.3 nm, demonstrating its graphitic nature (high sp2-carbon content) similar to those reported for carbon-based quantum dots [56]. Obviously, unlike other ZIF-8 known to have rhombic dodecahedron morphology, the prepared ZIF-L possesses leaf-like morphology, as shown in Fig. 3d [61]. After the encapsulation of NCDs to prepare NCDs@ZIF-L nanocomposite, a 2D framework can be clearly observed with an approximate length of 4 µm (Fig. 3e). Clearly, from Fig. 3e, the obtained NCDs@ZIF-L nanocomposite exhibits rough surfaces, which are beneficial for improving their performance towards adsorption of PA. This explains that the incorporation of the NCDs into the ZIF-L changes the morphology without affecting the crystal structure, as explained later in XRD. As shown in Fig. 3f, a broad diffraction peak for NCDs was observed at 2ϴ = 24.5°, which is related to the (002) plane of graphitic carbon [63]. This could be due to the abundant functional groups on the surface of carbon dots, increasing their amorphous nature [39]. According to Bragg's Equation [56], we determine the interplanar spacing (d) of (002) plane as 0.34 nm. Several XRD patterns are observed for ZIF-L at 2ϴ = 7.2, 10.7, 13.5, 15, 17, 18, 20.5, 21.6, 24.5, 27.7, 29.5, 30.7, 31.3 and 32.6° attributed to (110), (200), (211), (220), (310), (222), (321), (411), (332), (422), (431), (440), (433) and (442), respectively, which is much consistent with other studies [25,64]. On the other hand, the NCDs@ZIF-L nanocomposite possesses similar diffraction patterns with different reflection planes, showing no significant change in the XRD pattern after encapsulation of NCDs, which does not affect the crystalline structure of the ZIF-L [39]. The low concentration of NCDs showed a very weak peak that is unnoticeable compared to strong peaks of ZIF-L. These results confirm the formation of a high crystalline NCDs@ZIF-L nanocomposite even after encapsulation of carbon dots [25].

FTIR spectra were collected to determine the functional groups present in NCDs, ZIF-L, and NCDs@ZIF-L nanocomposite framework (Fig. S2). Different peaks are observed for NCDs, in which the peaks that appeared at 1400 and 1634 cm−1 are related to the stretching vibration of C=C and C=O stretching bonds, indicating the presence of alkyl and aryl groups. Two characteristic peaks are also observed at 2920 and 3445 cm−1 for the C−H and O−H stretching vibrations, respectively. Furthermore, a peak at 3212 cm−1 is assignable to the N–H stretching vibration in NCDs. These peaks suggest that the resultant carbon dots possess oxygen and nitrogen functional groups [65]. As shown in Fig. S2, ZIF-L shows two peaks appearing at 755 and 682 cm−1, which are assignable to the out-of-plane sp 2 aromatic C−H bending [21,60]. Two characteristic peaks located at 1147 and 1000 cm−1, can also be ascribed to the aromatic C–N stretching bond of the imidazole units and the in-plane bending of the ring, respectively [64,66]. Furthermore, the peaks observed at 1429 and 1590 cm−1 correspond to the stretching vibration of the imidazole ring and C=N stretch bond, respectively [21,25]. Two broad characteristic peaks located at 2925 and 3120 cm−1 are attributed to C−H stretching modes of the imidazole ring and methyl groups of the organic linker [25]. The broad peak located at 3226 cm−1 is assignable to the N−H stretching bond due to protonated imidazole [21]. Furthermore, the encapsulation of carbon dots into the ZIF-L leaf was studied by FTIR (Fig. S2). As clearly seen, no major changes were observed upon encapsulation of NCDs into ZIF-L. Most of ZIF-L peaks can be clearly recognized in the FTIR spectrum, in addition to some other characteristic peaks of NCDs located at 1637, 3212, and 3434 cm−1 for C=O stretching bond, N-H stretching vibration, and O−H stretch vibrations of NCDs, which is highly intensified in the nanocomposite [66]. The encapsulation of NCDs in the ZIF-L might occur due to the interaction between Zn2+ ions and the carboxylic/nitrogen-containing groups on the surface of NCDs during the growth of nanocomposite [67]. In previous reports, the morphology of ZIF-L could influence the N2 gas adsorption [68]. Accordingly, ZIF-L displayed a type I isotherm with a steep increase in N2 uptake at a low relative pressure, indicating its high degree of microporosity in the nanoleaf structures [48]. Therefore, our synthesized ZIF-L exhibited a high BET surface area and a large total pore volume, as previously reported for 2D ZIF-8 nanoleaf MOF [42]. Although adding NCDs would relatively reduce the surface area of the nanocomposite, the obtained NCDs@ZIF-L nanocomposite can provide more attachment active sites for analyte enrichment. This photoluminescent structural arrangement system with high microporosity is favorable and allows the reactant to contact the core active site for utilization in the detection of PA, as their microporous nature is accessible to various targeted guest molecules.

The concentration of PA at trace levels was determined using the NCDs@ZIF-L nanocomposite framework. Before PA detection, the intensity of fluorescence emission was demonstrated in different solvents and water to study the effects of the liquid medium on the fluorescence of the nanocomposite (Fig. S3). The fluorescence spectra were found to be greatly dependent on different organic solvents. It was noted that as the solvent polarity decreased, the stability of the NCDs@ZIF-L suspensions decreased dramatically. In non-polar solvents like hexane and toluene, the leaf nanocomposite quickly aggregates. Colloidal suspensions remained stable in medium-polar solvents such as water, THF, ethanol, methanol, and Ac (Fig. S3). Among the investigated solvents, only water could disperse the nanocomposite for extended periods of time, which was attributed to the Coulomb stabilization of the nanoparticles in water. As shown in Fig. S3, the normalized fluorescence spectra of the suspensions in water, THF, ethanol, methanol, and Ac show that the solvent has only a minor effect on the emission spectra of the nanocomposite, implying that the emission does not originate from solvation effects but is most likely due to the structure of nanocomposite. However, in toluene, the fluorescence intensity diminishes dramatically due to the quenching effect. In this regard, different concentrations of PA in the range of 0–5 μM were added to an aqueous solution of the nanocomposite (500 µL, 4 mg mL−1) at pH 7, and the fluorescence intensity was then measured at an excitation wavelength of λex = 360 nm (Fig. 4 a). It is well-known that photoluminescence occurs due to electronic transitions from the lowest unoccupied to the highest occupied excited states. Under excitation at 360 nm, the fluorescence spectrum of the NCDs@ZIF-L nanocomposite contains a main emission peak at 453 nm (Fig. 4a). The fluorescence quenching behavior increases with increasing the concentration of PA, as shown in Fig. 4a. The fluorescence quenching behavior exhibits an increase with the rising concentration of PA, as depicted in Fig. 4a. The increase in fluorescence quenching of the NCDs@ZIF-L nanocomposite was described by Stern-Volmer equation Eq. (2) to demonstrate its sensing affinity towards picric acid, with a remarkably low limit of detection (LOD) of 0.139 μM at room temperature (Fig. 4b). The LOD was calculated from the slope of the calibration curve (y = 0.99 + 0.060x, R 2 = 0.9913) utilizing the data from Fig. 4b and Eq. (2). Conversely, the fluorescence quenching effect of PA on pure NCDs was also measured (Fig. S4). The Stern-Volmer plot demonstrated a limit of detection (LOD) of 0.77 μM for NCDs. The higher sensitivity of the composite system allows for the utilization of the NCDs@ZIF-L nanocomposite to determine traces of PA in solution, with an exceedingly low LOD.

In aqueous solutions, the hydroxyl group of PA plays a crucial role in the interaction with the surface-active sites-rich-electron nanospaces of the NCDs@ZIF-L nanocomposite. In other words, the NCDs@ZIF-L nanocomposite with rough surface synergistically combines active-rich-electron nanospaces with a high surface area, both of which are particularly beneficial for selective detection of hazardous PA because of reinforced hydrogen bonding interactions with the hydroxyl groups of PA aromatic rings. The synergistic effects of high nanoporosity of ZIF-L and NCDs created a promising nanocomposite-sensing system suitable for monitoring hazardous PA. This interaction might increase the fluorescence quenching of the NCDs@ZIF-L nanocomposite, resulting in a system having good selectivity and high sensitivity towards PA. The interaction was further investigated through an overlap between the absorption spectrum of PA and the fluorescence emission of the NCDs@ZIF-L nanocomposite (Fig. 5 ). The spectral overlap suggested the quenching mechanism might be accomplished within fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) [69]. Our NCDs@ZIF-L nanocomposite shows a high sensing affinity toward PA compared to previously reported materials (Table S2).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selective study and real samples detection

ce:section.ce:para: We successfully prepared NCDs@ZIF-L rod-like nanocomposite having high quantum yield reached 84 % through encapsulation of NCDs (6 nm) synthesized from citric acid and ethylene diamine and ZIF-L nanoleaf using a green approach in aqueous media at room temperature. Unlike ZIF-8, ZIF-L was synthesized in the form of a semi-sodalite structure containing free organic linkers linked through hydrogen bonds; thus, more active sites on the surface of the ZIF-L were expected to be obtained. The synthesized nanocomposite exhibited a remarkable increase in its quenching, demonstrating its relatively high sensitivity and selectivity towards PA despite the presence of other analytes interferences. The NCDs@ZIF-L nanocomposite with rough surface synergistically combines active-rich-electron nanospaces with a high surface area, both were particularly beneficial for selective detection of hazardous PA because of reinforced hydrogen bonding interactions with hydroxyl groups of PA aromatic rings. The synergistic effects of high nanoporosity of ZIF-L and N-containing carbon quantum dots created a promising 2D nanocomposite-sensing system suitable for monitoring hazardous PA. The NCDs@ZIF-L nanocomposite showed high sensing affinity for determining traces of PA in solution with a very LOD of 0.139 μM at room temperature. Our NCDs@ZIF-L nanocomposite shows a high sensing affinity toward PA compared to previously reported materials (Table S3). The NCDs@ZIF-L sensing system demonstrated its distinguished performance in determining PA in real samples, demonstrating its applicability in realistic applications, which can be applied to efficiently treat water contaminated with traces of PA in the future.
